body_text:
Section 5.1 Cauchy's Residue Theorem

EXAMPLE 1 An application of the residue theorem
Let $C$ be a simple closed positively oriented path such that $1,-i$, and $i$ are in interior of $C$ and -1 is in the exterior of $C$ (Figure 2). Find
$$
\int_{C} \frac{d z}{z^{4}-1}
$$

Solution The function $f(z)=\frac{1}{z^{4}-1}$ has isolated singularities at $z= \pm 1$ and Three of these are inside $C$, and according to (2) we have
$$
\int_{C} \frac{d z}{z^{4}-1}=2 \pi i(\operatorname{Res}(1)+\operatorname{Res}(i)+\operatorname{Res}(-i))
$$

To compute the residues, we will find the Laurent series of $f(z)$ around each isolat singularity and obtain the residue as the coefficient $a_{-1}$ in each case. We ha $z^{4}-1=(z-1)(z+1)(z-i)(z+i)$, so $\pm 1$ and $\pm i$ are simple roots of the polynom $z^{4}-1=0$. Hence $f(z)=\frac{1}{z^{4}-1}$ has simple poles at $\pm 1$ and $\pm i$ (Theorem 7( Section 4.6). Let $z_{0}$ denote any one of the points $1, \pm i$. Because $z_{0}$ is a simple po $f$ has a Laurent series expansion around $z_{0}$ of the form

the
1.
$$
f(z)=\frac{a_{-1}}{z-z_{0}}+a_{0}+a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)+a_{2}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}+\cdots
$$

So
$$
\left(z-z_{0}\right) f(z)=a_{-1}+\left(z-z_{0}\right) h(z)
$$
where $h(z)=a_{0}+a_{1}\left(z-z_{0}\right)+a_{2}\left(z-z_{0}\right)^{2}+\cdots$ is analytic at $z_{0}$. Taking limits $z \rightarrow z_{0}$ and using $\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) h(z)=0$, we obtain the useful formula
$$
\lim _{z \rightarrow z_{0}}\left(z-z_{0}\right) f(z)=a_{-1}=\operatorname{Res}\left(z_{0}\right)
$$
which can be used to compute the residue of $f$ at a simple pole. Using the facto ization $z^{4}-1=(z-1)(z+1)(z-i)(z+i)$, we have at $z_{0}=1$
$$
\begin{aligned}
\operatorname{Res}(1) & =\lim _{z \rightarrow 1}(z-1) \frac{1}{z^{4}-1}=\lim _{z \rightarrow 1} \frac{1}{(z+1)(z-i)(z+i)} \\
& =\left.\frac{1}{(z+1)(z-i)(z+i)}\right|_{z=1}=\frac{1}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Similarly, at $z_{0}=i$, we have
$$
\operatorname{Res}(i)=\lim _{z \rightarrow i}(z-i) \frac{1}{z^{4}-1}=\left.\frac{1}{(z-1)(z+1)(z+i)}\right|_{z=i}=\frac{i}{4}
$$
and at $z=-i$,
$$
\operatorname{Res}(-i)=\lim _{z \rightarrow-1}(z+i) \frac{1}{z^{4}-1}=\left.\frac{1}{(z-1)(z+1)(z-i)}\right|_{z=-1}=-\frac{i}{4}
$$

Plugging these values into (3), we obtain
$$
\int_{C} \frac{d z}{z^{4}-1}=\frac{\pi i}{2}
$$
